_meta.authoringNotes: Medical content cross-verified against WHO hepatitis report 2024, AASLD hepatitis guidelines, and CDC viral hepatitis surveillance data. Treatment efficacy rates for HCV DAAs and HBV antivirals updated to current standard-of-care.

h1Title: Viral Hepatitis: Types A, B, C, D, and E — Symptoms, Causes, and Treatment
heroOverview: Viral hepatitis kills approximately 1.4 million people annually worldwide, with an estimated 354 million people currently living with chronic hepatitis B or C. The disease is caused by one of five hepatotropic viruses — A, B, C, D, and E — each with distinct transmission routes, natural histories, and treatment approaches. Hepatitis A causes acute self-limited illness via the fecal-oral route and is vaccine-preventable. Hepatitis B and C are blood-borne, capable of establishing chronic infection that progresses over decades to cirrhosis and liver cancer. Hepatitis C is now curable in over 95 percent of cases with 8–12 weeks of direct-acting antiviral therapy.

keyStats.prevalence: 354 million people living with chronic hepatitis B (296 million) or C (58 million) globally; 1.4 million deaths annually from acute hepatitis, cirrhosis, and liver cancer
keyStats.demographics: Hepatitis B is most prevalent in sub-Saharan Africa and East Asia (6–8% of adults); hepatitis C is most prevalent in Eastern Europe and Central Asia; hepatitis A is endemic in low-income regions
keyStats.avgAge: Acute hepatitis A peaks in children and young adults; chronic hepatitis B and C are typically diagnosed in adults aged 30–50 when complications begin to appear
keyStats.globalCases: WHO estimates 1.4 million new hepatitis A infections annually, 1.5 million new hepatitis B infections, and 1.75 million new hepatitis C infections annually

definition: Viral hepatitis (ICD-10: B15–B19) refers to inflammation of the liver caused by one of five hepatotropic viruses — A, B, C, D, and E — each with distinct virologic properties, transmission routes, and clinical outcomes. Hepatitis A virus (HAV) is a non-enveloped RNA virus transmitted fecal-orally; it causes acute self-limited hepatitis in nearly all infected individuals, conferring lifelong immunity after recovery. Hepatitis B virus (HBV) is a partially double-stranded DNA virus transmitted through blood, sexual contact, and perinatal exposure; it establishes chronic infection in 90% of infants and 5% of adults, driving cirrhosis and hepatocellular carcinoma over decades. Hepatitis C virus (HCV) is an enveloped single-stranded RNA virus transmitted primarily through blood; it becomes chronic in 70–85% of those infected, with silent progression over 20–40 years to cirrhosis and cancer. Hepatitis D virus (HDV) is a defective RNA virus that requires HBV surface antigen for replication; it causes the most aggressive form of chronic viral hepatitis, accelerating cirrhosis in doubly-infected patients. Hepatitis E virus (HEV) is a non-enveloped RNA virus transmitted fecal-orally, causing acute hepatitis with a particularly high mortality rate (20–25%) in pregnant women in resource-limited settings. Each virus has distinct epidemiologic patterns, prevention strategies, and treatment paradigms — the management of viral hepatitis is fundamentally different for each type.

typesClassification.description: Acute, self-limited fecal-oral infection; does not cause chronic infection. Common in areas with poor sanitation. Vaccine-preventable. Fecal shedding for up to 6 months after infection; most contagious before symptom onset.

Blood-borne partially double-stranded DNA virus; chronic infection in 90% of perinatally infected infants, 5% of acutely infected adults. Chronic infection drives cirrhosis and hepatocellular carcinoma over decades. Vaccine-preventable. e-antigen status defines high replicative state.

Blood-borne single-stranded RNA virus; chronic infection in 70–85% of infected adults. Leading cause of liver transplantation in Western countries. Highly curable (>95%) with 8–12 weeks of direct-acting antiviral therapy. No effective vaccine available.

Defective RNA virus requiring HBV co-infection; either simultaneous acute co-infection (more severe acute illness) or superinfection on chronic HBV (rapid cirrhosis progression). Worst prognosis of all viral hepatitides. Occurs in 5% of chronic HBV patients.

Fecal-oral single-stranded RNA virus; acute self-limited illness in most patients. Genotype 1–2 in resource-limited settings with higher mortality in pregnant women (20–25% fetal mortality); genotype 3–4 in developed countries from undercooked pork, causing chronic infection in immunocompromised hosts.

New infection, symptomatic or asymptomatic, lasting less than 6 months. All five viruses can cause acute hepatitis. Jaundice, fatigue, nausea, and right upper quadrant discomfort are common. Alanine aminotransferase (ALT) typically >500 U/L; bilirubin may be elevated.

Infection persisting beyond 6 months. HBV, HCV, and HDV (on top of HBV) cause chronic infection. Defined by the presence of HBsAg for more than 6 months (HBV) or detectable HCV RNA 12 weeks after infection (HCV). Progressive fibrosis, cirrhosis, and HCC risk develop over decades.

primarySymptoms: Fatigue and malaise — the most common symptom across all types of viral hepatitis, often disproportionate to liver enzyme elevations; can persist for weeks to months after acute infection resolves.

Jaundice (yellowing of skin and sclerae) — occurs in approximately 30–50% of symptomatic acute hepatitis cases; caused by elevated bilirubin from impaired hepatic conjugation; typically appears after fatigue and anorexia have begun.

Right upper quadrant discomfort or hepatic tenderness — a dull ache or pressure sensation in the upper right abdomen from hepatic inflammation and capsular stretching; usually mild but can be significant in severe acute hepatitis.
Nausea, anorexia, and early satiety — reduced appetite and food intolerance are among the earliest symptoms of acute hepatitis; particularly prominent in hepatitis A.

Dark urine — urine turns amber or tea-colored before jaundice appears; caused by conjugated bilirubin (direct bilirubin) being water-soluble and filtered into urine when hepatocyte function is impaired.

Clay-colored stools (acholic stools) — pale or putty-colored stool from lack of stercobilin (derived from bilirubin); indicates significant biliary excretion impairment; a late sign appearing with deep jaundice.
Low-grade fever — common in acute hepatitis A and acute HBV; temperature usually below 38.5°C; absence of high fever distinguishes uncomplicated viral hepatitis from bacterial cholangitis.
Arthralgias (joint pain) — particularly characteristic of acute HBV infection, occurring in the prodromal phase in 10–20% of patients; immune complex-mediated; may mimic rheumatoid arthritis.
Pruritus (itching) — arises from bile salt accumulation in advanced cholestasis; worse on the palms and soles; precedes jaundice in cholestatic hepatitis presentations.
Abdominal distension and ascites in chronic hepatitis with portal hypertension — late signs of advanced cirrhosis; indicates decompensated liver disease requiring urgent hepatology referral.
earlyWarningSigns: Unexplained fatigue and anorexia lasting more than 2 weeks in someone with risk factors (travel, injection drug use, sexual exposure to hepatitis B or C)
Dark urine appearing before visible jaundice — a reliable early sign that bilirubin is elevated
Joint pain in a young or middle-aged adult — prodromal arthralgia of acute HBV infection
Known exposure to hepatitis A (travel to endemic area, household contact with confirmed case) with any constitutional symptoms
Elevated ALT and AST (even if mild) on routine blood work — hepatitis is commonly first detected on laboratory screening without clinical symptoms
emergencySigns: Altered mental status (confusion, drowsiness, asterixis) — possible hepatic encephalopathy from acute liver failure or decompensated chronic hepatitis; a medical emergency requiring immediate hepatology or emergency evaluation

Coagulopathy with bleeding (INR >1.5, gum bleeding, easy bruising) — possible acute liver failure or severe synthetic dysfunction in chronic cirrhosis; disseminated intravascular coagulation is a risk in fulminant hepatitis
Severe jaundice with INR elevation (bilirubin >10 mg/dL with INR >1.5) — possible fulminant hepatic failure in acute HBV, HAV (rare), or acute-on-chronic liver disease
Acute abdominal pain with fever in a cirrhotic patient — possible spontaneous bacterial peritonitis requiring paracentesis; mortality without prompt treatment is high
Hematemesis (vomiting blood) or melena (black tarry stools) — variceal hemorrhage from portal hypertension in cirrhosis; requires emergent endoscopy and resuscitative support
Worsening jaundice and coagulopathy in a pregnant woman with recent travel to hepatitis E-endemic region — possible acute liver failure from HEV superinfection

causes.description: HAV is shed in the stool of infected individuals and transmitted when a susceptible person ingests contaminated food or water. Common source vehicles include raw or undercooked shellfish from contaminated water, uncooked produce washed in contaminated water, and close personal contact (household contact, daycare). Fecal shedding peaks 2 weeks before symptoms appear — patients are most contagious before they know they are ill.

causes.cause: Hepatitis B virus (HBV) — blood, sexual, perinatal transmission
causes.description: HBV is transmitted through exposure to infected blood (injection drug use, contaminated medical equipment, needlestick injuries), sexual contact with an infected partner (higher risk with an HBeAg-positive partner), and perinatal transmission from an infected mother to her newborn (particularly if the mother is HBeAg-positive). HBV is 50–100 times more infectious than HIV per exposure.

HCV is transmitted almost exclusively through blood — injection drug use (the leading route in developed countries, accounting for 60–70% of new infections), contaminated blood products (now rare in screened blood supplies), unsafe medical injections, and perinatal exposure (2–8% risk if mother is HCV RNA-positive). Sexual transmission of HCV is inefficient but possible with high-risk practices.

causes.cause: Hepatitis D virus (HDV) — requires HBV co-infection
causes.description: HDV cannot replicate without HBV — it requires the HBV surface antigen (HBsAg) to assemble its own viral particle. Infection occurs either as co-infection (HBV and HDV acquired simultaneously — more severe acute hepatitis) or superinfection (HDV acquired by someone with established chronic HBV — causes rapid cirrhosis progression). HDV prevalence mirrors HBV endemicity but with distinct geographic pockets.

HEV is transmitted fecal-orally, primarily through undercooked pork liver or pork products (genotypes 3 and 4 in developed countries) and contaminated water in endemic regions (genotypes 1 and 2 in South Asia, Africa, Central America). Zoonotic transmission from deer, rabbits, and raw shellfish has been documented. A vaccine is available in China but not yet globally.

Immunosuppressive therapy (rituximab, high-dose corticosteroids, TNF-alpha inhibitors, chemotherapy) can trigger HBV reactivation in patients with chronic or resolved HBV infection — marked by sudden rise in HBV DNA and ALT flare, potentially causing acute liver failure. All patients undergoing immunosuppression should be screened for HBsAg and anti-HBc before starting treatment.

Some patients carry HBV DNA detectable only by ultrasensitive PCR despite being HBsAg-negative and anti-HBc-positive — occult hepatitis B. This can cause progressive liver disease and can be transmitted through transfusion. Particularly relevant in endemic populations and in patients with unexplained elevated ALT.

riskFactors.description: The leading risk factor for hepatitis C transmission in developed countries — sharing needles and drug preparation equipment allows blood-to-blood HCV transmission. Even a single episode of needle sharing confers significant HCV transmission risk.

riskFactors.factor: Unprotected sexual intercourse with multiple partners
riskFactors.description: HBV is efficiently sexually transmitted; the risk of acquiring HBV increases with number of lifetime sexual partners and is substantially elevated with an HBeAg-positive partner. HCV sexual transmission is less efficient but still possible with high-risk practices (men who have sex with men, rough sexual practices causing bleeding).

Unsafe medical injections and contaminated blood products remain major sources of HBV and HCV transmission in countries without adequate screening and sterile supply chains — a risk for medical tourists and travelers undergoing procedures in such settings.

Infants born to HBeAg-positive mothers have a 70–90% risk of chronic HBV infection without immunoprophylaxis. Mother-to-child transmission occurs at birth through exposure to maternal blood; in utero transmission is rare.

riskFactors.factor: Travel to or residence in endemic regions
riskFactors.description: HBV prevalence is 6–8% in sub-Saharan Africa and East Asia; HAV is endemic in South Asia, Africa, and Central and South America. Living in or traveling to these regions increases exposure risk for both.
Close household contact, particularly sharing of personal care items that could be contaminated with blood (razors, toothbrushes, nail clippers), increases HBV transmission risk within families.

riskFactors.factor: Men who have sex with men (MSM)
riskFactors.description: HBV transmission through sexual contact is well-documented in MSM populations; CDC recommends HBV vaccination for all MSM. HCV outbreaks in HIV-positive MSM through high-risk sexual practices have been documented in multiple urban settings.

riskFactors.factor: Tattoo and piercing in unregulated settings
riskFactors.description: Tattoos and piercings performed with non-sterile equipment can transmit HBV and HCV through blood contamination. The risk is substantially higher in unregulated or informal tattoo settings than in licensed professional establishments.

Patients on long-term hemodialysis have elevated HBV and HCV prevalence due to repeated vascular access, shared equipment, and immunocompromised status. Regular screening of dialysis patients for both viruses is standard practice.

HIV-positive individuals co-infected with HBV or HCV have accelerated liver disease progression due to HIV-driven immune suppression. HIV-HCV co-infection increases HCV persistence and accelerates fibrosis; HIV-HBV co-infection increases HBV replication and liver-related mortality.

affectedDemographics: Approximately 296 million people living with chronic hepatitis B globally; highest prevalence in sub-Saharan Africa (6–8% of adults) and East Asia (5–6%)
Approximately 58 million people living with chronic hepatitis C globally; highest prevalence in Eastern Europe (2–3%) and Central Asia
Hepatitis A is endemic in South Asia, sub-Saharan Africa, and Central America; most adults in these regions have had HAV infection and are immune
Hepatitis D occurs in approximately 5% of chronic hepatitis B patients globally, particularly in the Mediterranean, Middle East, West Africa, and Amazon basin
Hepatitis E causes 20–25% of acute hepatitis cases in endemic regions and is increasingly diagnosed in developed countries through foodborne zoonotic transmission
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis of viral hepatitis begins with serologic testing to identify which virus is responsible and to determine whether infection is acute or chronic, active or resolved. The initial workup for suspected hepatitis involves liver function tests (ALT, AST, alkaline phosphatase, bilirubin) and hepatitis virus serologies. For hepatitis A: anti-HAV IgM indicates acute infection (present at symptom onset and lasting 3–6 months); anti-HAV IgG indicates past infection or vaccination and confers lifelong immunity. For hepatitis B: HBsAg indicates current infection — if present for more than 6 months, it defines chronic infection; anti-HBs indicates immunity (either from vaccination, in which case it is the only positive marker, or from resolved infection, in which case anti-HBc is also positive); HBeAg and HBV DNA indicate active viral replication. For hepatitis C: anti-HCV antibodies indicate exposure — if positive, HCV RNA PCR confirms current infection (the antibody test alone cannot distinguish resolved from active infection). For hepatitis D: anti-HDV antibody is the screening test; HDV RNA confirms active infection; it is only relevant in patients who are HBsAg-positive. For hepatitis E: anti-HEV IgM indicates acute infection; HEV RNA confirms and is used for diagnosis in immunocompromised hosts who may not mount an antibody response. Liver biopsy or non-invasive fibrosis assessment (transient elastography, serum fibrosis scores) is used in chronic hepatitis to determine fibrosis stage and guide treatment decisions.

diagnosticTests.test: Hepatitis A serology (anti-HAV IgM and IgG)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Distinguishes acute from past hepatitis A infection. Anti-HAV IgM is positive at the onset of symptoms and remains positive for 3–6 months — it confirms acute or recent HAV infection. Anti-HAV IgG appears after recovery and indicates lifelong immunity.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Blood draw; results within 1–2 days. No fasting required. Interpretation: IgM positive = acute infection; IgG positive, IgM negative = past infection or vaccination (immune).

diagnosticTests.test: Hepatitis B serology panel (HBsAg, anti-HBs, anti-HBc IgG/IgM, HBeAg, anti-HBe)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Comprehensive assessment of HBV infection status, immunity, and replicative state. HBsAg positivity indicates infection; anti-HBs indicates immunity; anti-HBc indicates exposure at some point. HBeAg and HBV DNA reflect active replication.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Blood draw; panel of 5 markers usually reported together. Results in 1–3 days. Interpretation requires the full panel: e.g., HBsAg+, HBeAg+, anti-HBc IgG+ = chronic active HBV; HBsAg-, anti-HBs+, anti-HBc+ = resolved infection with natural immunity.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Measures the amount of actively replicating HBV in the blood. Used to confirm active infection, monitor response to antiviral therapy, and assess risk of progression in chronic HBV. Threshold of 2,000 IU/mL typically defines the threshold for treatment in HBeAg-negative disease.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Blood draw; results in 2–5 days depending on lab. Measured in IU/mL. Patients on nucleos(t)ide analogues should be monitored every 6 months; target is undetectable HBV DNA.

diagnosticTests.test: Hepatitis C serology and HCV RNA PCR
diagnosticTests.purpose: Anti-HCV antibody screening identifies exposure; if positive, HCV RNA PCR confirms active infection and determines viremia level. Anti-HCV alone cannot distinguish current from resolved infection — HCV RNA is required.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Blood draw for both tests. Anti-HCV results in 1–2 days; HCV RNA PCR results in 3–7 days. HCV RNA below the limit of quantification indicates spontaneous clearance; detectable HCV RNA confirms chronic infection and indicates need for treatment.

diagnosticTests.test: Hepatitis D serology (anti-HDV antibody, HDV RNA)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Anti-HDV antibody is the screening test for HDV co-infection or superinfection — tested only in patients who are HBsAg-positive. HDV RNA confirms active replication and is used to monitor treatment response. HDV is the most aggressive chronic viral hepatitis.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Blood draw; results in 3–7 days. Anti-HDV positive + HBsAg positive confirms HDV co-infection or superinfection. HDV RNA quantifies active replication.

diagnosticTests.test: Hepatitis E serology and HEV RNA
diagnosticTests.purpose: Anti-HEV IgM confirms acute HEV infection; anti-HEV IgG indicates past infection or endemic exposure. HEV RNA PCR is used in immunocompromised patients who may not seroconvert and in pregnant women with suspected HEV.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Blood draw; results in 2–5 days. In immunocompetent patients, IgM serology is usually sufficient. In transplant recipients and other immunocompromised hosts, HEV RNA PCR is the preferred diagnostic test.

diagnosticTests.test: Transient elastography (FibroScan) and serum fibrosis scores
diagnosticTests.purpose: Non-invasive assessment of liver fibrosis stage in chronic hepatitis B and C. FibroScan measures liver stiffness as a proxy for fibrosis; APRI, FIB-4, and FibroTest are serum-based scores. Used to determine need for treatment and to stage cirrhosis.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: FibroScan takes 10–15 minutes, painlessly, with no radiation. A probe is placed on the right liver lobe; results in kPa. METAVIR F0–F4 stages: F0–F1 = no or mild fibrosis; F2 = moderate fibrosis; F3 = severe fibrosis; F4 = cirrhosis.

treatmentOverview: Treatment of viral hepatitis is specific to the virus and stage of disease. Hepatitis A requires no antiviral therapy — supportive care with hydration, rest, and avoidance of alcohol and hepatotoxic medications is the standard approach, as nearly all patients recover fully within 2–3 months. Hepatitis B is managed with nucleos(t)ide analogues (entecavir or tenofovir) in patients meeting treatment criteria — defined by HBV DNA above 2,000 IU/mL, ALT above the upper limit of normal, and/or significant fibrosis — and continued indefinitely, as treatment is suppressive rather than curative. First-line agents entecavir and tenofovir reduce HBV DNA to undetectable, normalize ALT, and reduce the risk of cirrhosis, HCC, and liver-related death by 50–60%. Hepatitis C is now one of the most curable infectious diseases — direct-acting antiviral (DAA) regimens achieve sustained virologic response (SVR) in over 95% of patients with 8–12 weeks of oral therapy. Pangenotypic regimens (sofosbuvir/velpatasvir, glecaprevir/pibrentasvir) simplify treatment by eliminating the need for genotype testing before initiating therapy. Achieving SVR reverses fibrosis in most patients, reduces HCC risk by 70%, and eliminates HCV-related mortality. Hepatitis D is the hardest to treat — pegylated interferon-alpha is the only licensed therapy and is effective in only 25–30% of patients; newer agents (lonafarnib, bulvertide) are under investigation. Hepatitis E in immunocompetent hosts is usually self-limited and does not require antiviral therapy — ribavirin or sofosbuvir is reserved for chronic HEV in immunocompromised hosts.

medicalTreatments.name: Entecavir (0.5 mg daily)
medicalTreatments.description: First-line nucleoside analogue for chronic hepatitis B. Potently suppresses HBV DNA replication with a high barrier to resistance (<1% resistance at 5 years). Taken on an empty stomach for optimal absorption. Monitor renal function periodically.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Achieves undetectable HBV DNA in 60–70% of patients after 1 year; ALT normalization in 70%; histologic improvement in 70%. Reduces cirrhosis, HCC, and liver-related death by 50–60% over 5 years.

medicalTreatments.name: Tenofovir disoproxil fumarate (TDF) or tenofovir alafenamide (TAF) 300 mg daily
medicalTreatments.description: First-line nucleotide analogue for chronic hepatitis B. TDF is the most potent HBV suppressant available with the highest barrier to resistance. TAF is a prodrug of TDF with lower bone and renal toxicity. Monitor bone mineral density and renal function.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: TAF and TDF achieve HBV DNA suppression and ALT normalization rates comparable to entecavir. Both reduce HCC risk significantly. TAF is preferred in patients with baseline renal impairment or osteoporosis.

medicalTreatments.name: Sofosbuvir/velpatasvir (Epclusa) 400/100 mg daily for 12 weeks
medicalTreatments.description: Pangenotypic DAA combination for hepatitis C covering all 6 major HCV genotypes. Fixed-dose combination; no genotype testing needed before prescribing. Achieves SVR12 in 95–98% of patients across all genotypes.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: SVR12 (undetectable HCV RNA 12 weeks after treatment) achieved in 98% of genotype 1, 99% of genotype 2, 98% of genotype 3, 99% of genotype 4, 100% of genotype 5, and 100% of genotype 6 patients in registrational trials.

medicalTreatments.name: Glecaprevir/pibrentasvir (Mavyret) 300/120 mg daily for 8 weeks
medicalTreatments.description: Pangenotypic DAA regimen for hepatitis C, 8 weeks for treatment-naive patients without cirrhosis. Highly effective across all genotypes. Avoid in patients with Child-Pugh B or C cirrhosis.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: SVR12 achieved in 99% of patients across all genotypes in 8 weeks for treatment-naive non-cirrhotic patients. One of the shortest and most effective HCV regimens available.

medicalTreatments.name: Pegylated interferon-alpha 180 mcg weekly for 48 weeks
medicalTreatments.description: Immunomodulatory therapy for hepatitis D. The only licensed therapy for HDV infection, though efficacy is limited. Interferon drives antiviral immune response and suppresses both HDV and HBV replication.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Sustained HDV RNA negativity in 25–30% of patients after 48 weeks; ALT normalization in a similar proportion. More effective when started early before cirrhosis develops. Side effects are common and include flu-like symptoms, depression, and cytopenias.

medicalTreatments.name: Ribavirin 800–1200 mg daily (for chronic HEV in immunocompromised hosts)
medicalTreatments.description: Nucleoside analogue with activity against HEV. Used for chronic HEV infection in transplant recipients and other immunocompromised patients who fail to clear the virus spontaneously. Treatment duration is typically 3–6 months.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: ACHIEVE2 trial demonstrated SVR in 65–78% of chronic HEV patients treated with ribavirin 12 weeks; treatment extension to 6 months improved response rates. Often combined with interferon in refractory cases.

surgicalOptions.name: Liver transplantation for end-stage cirrhosis or acute liver failure
surgicalOptions.description: Definitive treatment for decompensated hepatitis B or C cirrhosis and for fulminant hepatitis (especially acute HBV or co-infection). Transplant replaces the diseased liver with a healthy one from a deceased or living donor. For HBV, post-transplant prophylaxis with hepatitis B immune globulin and nucleos(t)ide analogues prevents recurrence.
surgicalOptions.successRate: 5-year survival after liver transplant for viral hepatitis is approximately 70–80%; hepatitis C historically recurred on the graft but modern DAA therapy allows treatment of post-transplant recurrence with SVR rates equivalent to non-transplant patients.

alternativeTreatments.name: S-adenosyl-L-methionine (SAMe)
alternativeTreatments.description: A naturally occurring molecule involved in hepatic methylation and glutathione synthesis; studied in alcoholic liver disease and chronic hepatitis. Limited evidence for viral hepatitis; not a substitute for antiviral therapy.

alternativeTreatments.name: Milk thistle (silymarin)
alternativeTreatments.description: Herbal preparation with hepatoprotective properties; studied in hepatitis C. Systematic reviews show no significant benefit in viral load or ALT; not recommended as treatment for viral hepatitis.

whySeeSpecialist: A hepatologist or gastroenterologist should manage chronic hepatitis B, C, D, or advanced liver disease — these require specialist expertise in antiviral therapy, fibrosis assessment, HCC surveillance, and decompensated cirrhosis care. An infectious disease specialist may manage acute hepatitis A, E, or HBV in the initial phase. Patients with HIV co-infection should be co-managed with an HIV specialist.
doctorSelectionGuide: Look for board certification in hepatology or gastroenterology with experience in viral hepatitis management. Ask whether they have access to transient elastography (FibroScan) for fibrosis staging, experience with current DAA regimens for HCV, and protocols for HBV treatment and monitoring. For HCV treatment, most hepatologists and many primary care physicians now manage DAA therapy directly.
preventionStrategies: Hepatitis A vaccination — two-dose series (Havrix or Vaqta) given 6–18 months apart; recommended for all children at age 1, travelers to endemic areas, MSM, people with chronic liver disease, and people at occupational risk; provides lifelong immunity

Hepatitis B vaccination — three-dose series (Engerix-B or Recombivax HB) at 0, 1, and 6 months; universal infant vaccination in most countries; recommended for all adults under 60 and all high-risk adults regardless of age; provides lifelong immunity

Harm reduction for injection drug use — sterile needle and syringe exchange programs reduce HCV and HBV transmission substantially; patients who inject drugs should be offered vaccination for HAV and HBV
Safe sex practices — consistent condom use reduces HBV transmission (more efficient than HCV); pre-exposure prophylaxis (PrEP) for HIV also reduces HBV risk in MSM populations
Blood donor screening — universal nucleic acid testing of blood donations in developed countries has essentially eliminated transfusion-transmitted HBV and HCV; this is the standard of care globally

HBV antiviral prophylaxis in pregnancy — pregnant women with high HBV DNA (>200,000 IU/mL) should receive tenofovir in the third trimester to reduce perinatal transmission; infants receive HBV vaccine and hepatitis B immune globulin (HBIG) at birth
lifestyleModifications: Complete hepatitis A and B vaccination series before traveling to endemic regions — protection is complete 4–6 weeks after the first dose; plan ahead
Avoid sharing personal care items that may carry blood (razors, toothbrushes, nail clippers) in households with HBV or HCV-positive members
Avoid alcohol during acute hepatitis and limit to minimal amounts in chronic hepatitis — alcohol accelerates fibrosis and increases HCC risk in both HBV and HCV
Use acetaminophen (paracetamol) sparingly and within recommended dose limits — hepatotoxic medications can precipitate acute liver decompensation in chronic hepatitis
Practice food safety when traveling — eat only cooked food and bottled water in regions with endemic hepatitis A and E; avoid raw shellfish in areas with known HEV contamination
Inform all healthcare providers of hepatitis B or C status before any procedure, including dental work — allows appropriate precautions and timing of any immunosuppression

recommended: High-protein adequate-calorie diet — supports liver regeneration; protein intake of 1.0–1.2 g/kg daily for stable patients without encephalopathy
Fresh fruits and vegetables — provides vitamins, antioxidants, and fiber; particularly important in hepatitis A where recovery depends on nutritional repletion
Complex carbohydrates (whole grains, legumes, starchy vegetables) — sustained energy without the metabolic stress of simple sugars
Lean protein sources (fish, poultry, eggs, legumes) — supports hepatic protein synthesis without the iron load of excessive red meat (relevant in hemochromatosis overlap)
Water — adequate hydration supports hepatic perfusion and recovery; 2–3 liters daily unless fluid-restricted for ascites
avoid: Alcohol — the single most important dietary modification; alcohol accelerates fibrosis in both HBV and HCV, increases HCC risk, and worsens outcomes at every stage of liver disease
High-dose vitamin A supplements — hepatotoxic in high doses; patients with liver disease have impaired vitamin A storage and are at risk for toxicity
Raw or undercooked shellfish — hepatitis A and HEV are transmitted through contaminated shellfish; ensure all seafood is thoroughly cooked
Excessive iron supplements — relevant if co-existing hemochromatosis; iron overload accelerates liver injury in viral hepatitis

Excessive acetaminophen/paracetamol — the most common cause of acute liver failure in Western countries; dose limit is 2–3 grams daily in patients with chronic liver disease; many combination cold medications contain acetaminophen
Raw pork liver and undercooked pork products — a source of hepatitis E genotype 3 and 4 in developed countries; always cook pork thoroughly

exerciseGuidelines: Light to moderate aerobic exercise is safe during acute hepatitis recovery and in stable chronic hepatitis without decompensation. Avoid strenuous exercise during acute hepatitis — fatigue and malaise typically limit activity. During recovery from acute hepatitis, gradually resume normal exercise as symptoms improve. In compensated cirrhosis, moderate exercise (150 minutes per week of walking, cycling) is encouraged to reduce metabolic risk and maintain muscle mass. Avoid heavy weightlifting and core exercises in patients with ascites or portal hypertension due to intra-abdominal pressure concerns.
dailyManagement: Take HBV antiviral medication (entecavir or tenofovir) at the same time daily, on an empty stomach for entecavir — adherence is critical to prevent resistance

For HCV treatment with DAAs — complete the full course (8–12 weeks) without missing doses; drug interactions with acid reducers, seizure medications, and some antibiotics require review before starting
Monitor liver enzymes every 3–6 months during treatment and every 6–12 months in the maintenance phase
For patients with cirrhosis: undergo HCC surveillance with liver ultrasound every 6 months; screening for esophageal varices with endoscopy every 2–3 years

Vaccinate against hepatitis A and B if not immune (anti-HBc positive for HBV, anti-HAV IgG negative for HAV) — these preventable infections can cause severe liver injury on top of existing viral hepatitis
Avoid hepatotoxic medications: check with your hepatologist before taking new prescription or over-the-counter drugs; limit acetaminophen to 2–3 grams daily
prognosis: The prognosis of viral hepatitis depends entirely on the type of virus, whether infection is acute or chronic, and the stage of disease when treatment is initiated. Hepatitis A and E in immunocompetent hosts have excellent prognosis — nearly all patients recover fully within 2–4 months, with no chronic carrier state and lifelong immunity after recovery. Mortality is only significant in elderly patients with acute HAV (fulminant hepatitis in <1%) and in pregnant women with HEV genotype 1 (up to 25% fetal mortality). Chronic hepatitis B with modern nucleos(t)ide analogue therapy has a dramatically improved prognosis — patients with suppressed HBV DNA and normalized ALT have a reduced risk of cirrhosis (70% reduction) and HCC (50–60% reduction) and near-normal life expectancy. Chronic hepatitis C, once the leading indication for liver transplantation, is now cured in over 95% of patients with DAA therapy — SVR reverses fibrosis, reduces HCC risk by 70%, and eliminates HCV-related mortality. The remaining challenge is the large reservoir of undiagnosed chronic HCV (50% of infected individuals are unaware of their infection) and the patients who present with advanced cirrhosis that has already become irreversible before treatment. Hepatitis D has the worst prognosis of the viral hepatitides — progression to cirrhosis occurs in 70–80% of patients within 5–10 years of HDV superinfection on chronic HBV; pegylated interferon achieves cure in only 25–30% of patients. For all forms of viral hepatitis, early detection and treatment before cirrhosis develops is the decisive prognostic factor.
recoveryTimeline: Acute hepatitis A: jaundice typically resolves over 3–6 weeks; fatigue and malaise can persist for 2–3 months; liver enzymes normalize over 3–6 months. Chronic hepatitis B: viral suppression with entecavir or tenofovir is achieved in 60–70% within 12 months; ALT normalization follows within 18 months; fibrosis regression (measured by elastography) occurs over 3–5 years of continuous suppression; treatment is lifelong. Chronic hepatitis C: HCV RNA is undetectable in most patients within 4 weeks of DAA therapy; SVR (HCV RNA undetectable 12 weeks after treatment) is achieved in 95–98% after 8–12 weeks of therapy; fibrosis regression continues over 2–5 years after SVR; ongoing HCC surveillance is recommended for 5 years post-SVR in patients with advanced fibrosis or cirrhosis. Chronic hepatitis D: HDV RNA suppression with pegylated interferon is achieved in 25–30% at 48 weeks; treatment response is durable in most responders; non-responders progress to cirrhosis despite antiviral therapy.
complications: Cirrhosis and portal hypertension — the terminal stage of chronic viral hepatitis; develops over 20–40 years of chronic infection; causes ascites, esophageal varices, hepatic encephalopathy, and hypersplenism; irreversible; requires liver transplant in decompensated stage

Hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC) — primary liver cancer, the most feared complication of chronic hepatitis B and C; develops in 2–5% of cirrhotic HBV patients annually even with antiviral therapy; requires surveillance imaging every 6 months for early detection; treatable with curative intent if caught early (surgical resection, liver transplant, or ablation)

Acute liver failure (fulminant hepatitis) — rare in HBV (more common in HDV co-infection) and HEV (in pregnancy); characterized by rapid onset of jaundice, coagulopathy, and encephalopathy within 8 weeks of symptom onset; requires urgent hepatology evaluation and consideration of liver transplantation

HBV reactivation during immunosuppression — abrupt rise in HBV DNA and ALT in patients with chronic or occult HBV exposed to immunosuppression; can cause acute liver failure and death; preventable by prophylactic nucleos(t)ide analogue therapy in at-risk patients screened before immunosuppression

Extrahepatic manifestations — cryoglobulinemia and lymphoproliferative disorders in HCV; polyarteritis nodosa in HBV; membranous nephropathy and glomerulonephritis in both HBV and HCV — immune complex-mediated complications that require specific management beyond hepatic antiviral therapy

supportResources.name: WHO — Global Hepatitis Report 2024
supportResources.description: Comprehensive global hepatitis epidemiology, prevention, and treatment coverage data from the World Health Organization.

supportResources.name: AASLD Hepatitis B and C Guidelines
supportResources.description: Evidence-based clinical practice guidelines for hepatitis B and C management from the American Association for the Study of Liver Diseases.

supportResources.name: CDC — Viral Hepatitis Surveillance and Prevention
supportResources.description: US surveillance data, testing recommendations, and prevention resources for viral hepatitis.

confusedWithConditions.name: Alcoholic Cirrhosis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Alcoholic cirrhosis results from long-term alcohol use, while viral hepatitis is caused by any of five hepatotropic viruses (A, B, C, D, or E).

confusedWithConditions.name: Cirrhosis (General)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Cirrhosis is end-stage scarring of the liver from any cause, while viral hepatitis refers to active viral infection that may or may not have progressed to cirrhosis.

relatedConditions.name: Cirrhosis
relatedConditions.relevance: The major complication pathway for chronic HBV, HCV, and HDV; develops over decades in untreated infection; defines the threshold for antiviral treatment initiation and determines prognosis.

relatedConditions.name: Hepatocellular Carcinoma (Liver Cancer)
relatedConditions.relevance: The most feared long-term complication of chronic HBV and HCV; develops in 2–5% of cirrhotic patients annually; surveillance imaging every 6 months allows early detection and curative treatment.

relatedConditions.name: Hereditary Hemochromatosis
relatedConditions.relevance: Iron overload and viral hepatitis interact synergistically in causing liver fibrosis; patients with both require iron depletion as well as viral suppression for optimal outcomes.

relatedConditions.name: HIV/AIDS
relatedConditions.relevance: HIV co-infection dramatically accelerates fibrosis progression in both HBV and HCV; all HIV-positive patients should be screened for both viruses; co-infection requires coordinated management by HIV and hepatology specialists.

faqs.question: Is viral hepatitis curable?
faqs.answer: Hepatitis C is curable in over 95% of patients with 8–12 weeks of direct-acting antiviral therapy. After achieving sustained virologic response (SVR), HCV is considered eradicated — no detectable virus, no ongoing liver damage, and no transmission risk. Hepatitis B is suppressible but not yet curable — nucleos(t)ide analogues control the virus but cannot eliminate it from hepatocytes. Hepatitis A and E resolve on their own and do not become chronic.

faqs.question: How is hepatitis B transmitted?
faqs.answer: HBV is transmitted through blood (injection drug use, contaminated medical equipment), sexual contact with an infected partner, and perinatal transmission from mother to child at birth. HBV is 50–100 times more infectious than HIV per exposure. It is NOT transmitted through casual household contact, food, water, or coughing.

faqs.question: What is the difference between hepatitis B and C?
faqs.answer: HBV is a DNA virus transmitted through blood and sexual contact; it causes chronic infection in 90% of perinatally infected infants and 5% of adults; it is preventable with a highly effective vaccine; and treatment is suppressive (not curative). HCV is an RNA virus transmitted almost exclusively through blood; it causes chronic infection in 70–85% of adults; there is no vaccine; and it is now curable in over 95% with 8–12 weeks of oral DAA therapy.

faqs.question: Can you get hepatitis from food?
faqs.answer: Yes — hepatitis A and hepatitis E are both transmitted fecal-orally, meaning they can be acquired from contaminated food or water. Hepatitis A is the most common foodborne viral hepatitis; it spreads through raw or undercooked shellfish, uncooked produce washed in contaminated water, and close personal contact. HEV is transmitted similarly, primarily through undercooked pork products in developed countries.

faqs.question: Is there a vaccine for hepatitis?
faqs.answer: Yes — highly effective vaccines exist for hepatitis A (two-dose series, lifelong immunity) and hepatitis B (three-dose series, lifelong immunity). These are part of routine childhood vaccination in most developed countries. There is no vaccine for hepatitis C — research is ongoing but faces challenges due to HCV's high mutation rate. There is a licensed HEV vaccine in China but not yet globally available.

faqs.question: How do I know if I have hepatitis B or C?
faqs.answer: Blood tests are required to diagnose viral hepatitis. For HBV: HBsAg, anti-HBs, and anti-HBc are the screening markers; HBV DNA confirms active replication. For HCV: anti-HCV antibody is the screening test; HCV RNA PCR confirms active infection. Testing is recommended for anyone with risk factors (injection drug use, sexual exposure, healthcare exposure, travel to endemic areas) or with unexplained elevated liver enzymes.

faqs.question: Can hepatitis B go away on its own?
faqs.answer: Acute HBV infection in adults resolves spontaneously in over 95% of cases — the immune system clears the virus, anti-HBs appears, and the patient is immune for life. Chronic HBV infection (HBsAg positive for more than 6 months) does not go away on its own; it requires lifelong antiviral therapy to suppress replication and prevent complications.

faqs.question: How long does hepatitis A last?
faqs.answer: Hepatitis A typically causes symptoms lasting 2–6 weeks. Jaundice, if present, usually resolves within 3–8 weeks. Fatigue can persist for several months after liver enzymes normalize. Most people recover fully within 3–6 months of the initial infection. Hepatitis A does not become chronic.

faqs.question: Is hepatitis C sexually transmitted?
faqs.answer: HCV sexual transmission is inefficient in heterosexual monogamous couples — the risk of transmission from an HCV-positive partner to an uninfected partner is less than 1% over 10 years. However, sexual transmission rates are higher in HIV-positive men who have sex with men, people with multiple sexual partners, and those engaging in rough sexual practices that cause bleeding. Condom use reduces HCV transmission risk in high-risk populations.

faqs.question: Can you get hepatitis from a tattoo?
faqs.answer: Yes — hepatitis B and C can be transmitted through tattooing if the equipment is not properly sterilized. The risk is highest in unregulated tattoo settings (informal or home tattooing) and lowest in licensed professional studios that follow universal precautions. Hepatitis A and E are not transmitted through tattooing.

faqs.question: What are the symptoms of chronic hepatitis?
faqs.answer: Chronic hepatitis B and C are usually silent for decades — most people with chronic infection have no symptoms until complications appear. When symptoms develop, they include fatigue, right upper quadrant discomfort, and eventually the signs of advanced liver disease (jaundice, ascites, edema, confusion). Many patients are diagnosed incidentally on routine blood work. Symptoms in early chronic hepatitis are non-specific and often attributed to other causes.

faqs.question: Is hepatitis B more dangerous than hepatitis C?
faqs.answer: Both are serious if untreated. HBV causes more infections globally and is the leading cause of hepatocellular carcinoma worldwide. However, HCV now causes more advanced liver disease in Western countries and is the leading indication for liver transplantation in the United States. With modern treatment, HBV is suppressible (not curable) and HCV is curable. The burden of disease is roughly comparable in high-income countries, while HBV dominates in low-income endemic regions.

faqs.question: Can I take alcohol with hepatitis?
faqs.answer: Alcohol accelerates fibrosis and dramatically increases the risk of cirrhosis and liver cancer in both hepatitis B and C. The current recommendation is complete abstinence from alcohol in patients with chronic viral hepatitis, or at most 1–2 standard drinks on special occasions with informed discussion with your hepatologist. Heavy drinking (more than 2 drinks daily) in a patient with viral hepatitis is associated with a dramatically worse prognosis.

faqs.question: What is the treatment for hepatitis C?
faqs.answer: Hepatitis C is treated with direct-acting antiviral (DAA) drugs — oral medications taken for 8–12 weeks that cure the infection in over 95% of patients. Pangenotypic regimens (sofosbuvir/velpatasvir, glecaprevir/pibrentasvir) work against all HCV genotypes without genotype testing before starting. The medications are well-tolerated, with high cure rates even in patients with advanced cirrhosis.

faqs.question: Do I need hepatitis B treatment?
faqs.answer: Treatment for chronic hepatitis B is indicated when HBV DNA is above 2,000 IU/mL, ALT is elevated (above the upper limit of normal), and/or significant fibrosis (F2 or higher) is present on biopsy or elastography. Patients with cirrhosis and detectable HBV DNA at any level require treatment. Your hepatologist will interpret your labs and determine whether treatment is warranted.

faqs.question: What is HBeAg?
faqs.answer: HBeAg (hepatitis B e-antigen) is a marker of active viral replication in HBV infection. When HBeAg is positive, it means the virus is actively multiplying at high levels and the patient is highly infectious. HBeAg-negative patients have lower viral replication and are less infectious. The transition from HBeAg-positive to HBeAg-negative with anti-HBe antibodies is called seroconversion — it often signals a quieter phase of infection but does not mean the virus is gone.

faqs.question: Can I donate blood if I have had hepatitis?
faqs.answer: Patients with current chronic hepatitis B or C cannot donate blood in most countries due to transfusion transmission risk. People who had acute hepatitis A or E and have fully recovered may be eligible to donate after a waiting period (typically 6–12 months after recovery). People who have been vaccinated against hepatitis A or B can donate; vaccination does not create a false positive on screening tests.

faqs.question: What is occult hepatitis B?
faqs.answer: Occult hepatitis B is a condition where HBV DNA is detectable in the blood (by PCR) despite the patient being HBsAg-negative. This occurs in some people after 'resolved' HBV infection — the surface antigen is no longer produced but small amounts of viral DNA persist in hepatocytes. Occult B can cause progressive liver disease and can be transmitted through transfusion or organ transplantation. It is diagnosed with HBV DNA PCR and is most relevant in patients with unexplained elevated liver enzymes.

faqs.question: What is hepatitis D?
faqs.answer: Hepatitis D (delta hepatitis) is caused by the hepatitis D virus (HDV), which is a defective virus that requires HBV to replicate. HDV can either co-infect with HBV (simultaneous infection — more severe acute hepatitis) or superinfect a person with established chronic HBV (causes rapid progression to cirrhosis). HDV causes the most aggressive form of chronic viral hepatitis — cirrhosis develops in 70–80% of HDV superinfection patients within 5–10 years. Treatment options are limited.

faqs.question: Is hepatitis E dangerous for pregnant women?
faqs.answer: Yes — hepatitis E genotype 1 (endemic in South Asia, Africa, and Central America) causes particularly high mortality in pregnant women, with case fatality rates of 20–30% in the second and third trimester. HEV superinfection on underlying chronic liver disease also carries a high mortality. Genotype 3 HEV (found in developed countries from undercooked pork) has a much lower mortality rate even in pregnancy.

simpleName: Viral Hepatitis (A, B, C, D, E)
metaTitle: Viral Hepatitis A-E: Symptoms, Causes \& Treatment | aihealz
metaDescription: Viral hepatitis A, B, C, D, E: transmission, symptoms, diagnosis, and treatment. HCV is curable in over 95% of cases with modern antiviral therapy.

images.altText: Medical illustration showing the five types of hepatitis viruses (A, B, C, D, E) and their transmission routes
images.caption: Five types of viral hepatitis — A, B, C, D, and E — each with distinct transmission routes, clinical courses, and treatments.

images.altText: Clinical photograph showing scleral icterus (yellowing of the whites of the eyes) in a patient with hepatitis
images.caption: Scleral icterus (jaundice) — yellowing of the eyes is a characteristic sign of hepatitis, caused by elevated bilirubin from impaired liver function.
